summary: Gruber reviews Apple's 'Let Loose' iPad event, covering the new iPad Air (now available in 13-inch), the remarkably thin M4 iPad Pros, the Apple Pencil Pro, and the overall iPad lineup simplification. He emphasizes the M4 iPad Pro's unprecedented thinness (thinner than an iPod Nano at 5.1mm) and explains how the M4 chip was necessary to drive the new tandem OLED displays — a prime example of Apple's vertical integration advantage. He notes Apple came close to fulfilling his October 2023 wishlist for iPad lineup clarity, with the main miss being Face ID still absent from iPad Air. The piece also addresses Pencil proliferation concerns, pricing across the lineup, and various details like chip-binning and the nano-texture display option.
key_points: The M4 iPad Pro is thinner than an iPod Nano and up to 15% lighter, enabled specifically by the M4 chip's efficiency gains on TSMC's next-gen 3nm process
Apple's vertical integration — designing both the M4's display engine and the tandem OLED panels together — puts their products in a league of their own, as neither component works without the other
The iPad lineup is now much clearer with three tiers (iPad, Air, Pro) plus the 13-inch size option at both Air and Pro levels, though the iPad Mini remains an awkward outlier

Mark Gurman's pre-event M4 scoop was a 'hall of fame rumor scoop' that suggests M-series chip generations shouldn't be thought of as annual iterations but as parallel development for specific products
The Apple Pencil Pro's squeeze gesture and radial tool menu is a better design than the obvious upside-down-for-eraser approach because it provides shortcuts to all tools, not just one
money_quotes: This truly is one of those examples where Apple controlling the entire stack — their own silicon driving their own display design — puts their products in a league of their own.
They couldn't drive the new displays without the M4's display controller and they wouldn't have engineered that display controller if they hadn't had these tandem OLED displays in mind.
If you wanted a big-screen MacBook or iPad you had to pay premium Pro prices, even if you didn't need Pro features or performance.
It seems impossible that the new iPad Pros are thinner than an iPod Nano, but read the tech specs and weep.
One thing I'm taking away from this is that it's wrong to think about M-series generations as year-over-year annual iterations, like the A-series chips in iPhones.

The mistake that prevents Apple from being able to offer just one Pencil has nothing to do with any of the Pencil models, but instead was the company's yearslong resistance to moving the front-facing camera on iPads from the short side to the long side.
Anyone playing a drinking game that required taking a shot for each mention of 'AI' or 'machine learning' during the keynote probably passed out before it ended.
Apple is more comfortable rounding down than up. Underpromise, overdeliver.
key_insight: The M4 iPad Pro exemplifies Apple's vertical integration advantage: the chip and display were co-designed so neither works without the other, producing a device thinner than an iPod Nano that no competitor can replicate.
